item.title: Licensing
item.content:encoded: Since the original link was deleted, the license used here is CC BY 2.0. Link: http://web.archive.org/web/20180825213850/https://www.flickr.com/photos/rocketjim54/7469923240

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw a comment saying that this was similar to 579 but I think this is ALOT better and has a much more interesting way of showcasing the "fear of the unknown" than it does. You can still work out the basics of what happened from the titles alone and the idea of a cloud mass that literally disintegrates physical matter and encompassing the earth's surface is bloody scary.

It gets a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It never had fully written addenda except for the last one, though the format of that absence has changed to avoid raising reader expectations. I'm personally still quite happy with how the contextualization of implied events reads.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so did we go back to the previous version that has no addenda? because that's definitely how it reads now, an listicle of article titles with no real context. -1 until that gets fixed, and an even harder -1 if that's how it's supposed to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Judging by the comments, there was a previous version of the entry that had empty addenda. I would've hated that, but the way it's done now is great. Enthusiastic +1 for actually using the supposed secrecy of the Foundation!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, that escalated quickly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The planes with giant fans idea is… questionable, but otherwise I mostly agree. Ultimately, the story told just isn't that interesting - it's a stock-standard 'yeah so the world dissolved into chaos' which is unable to distinguish itself from other articles that might have used similar scenarios as a result of the bare-bones format choice. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The… The Day of Resurrection?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, planes with giant fans on them sound silly as hell.

Not to mention how unstable it would be, and it would be impossible to contain the recoil—causing the planes to drift backwards supersonic instead of pushing the clouds.

Also, it is physically impossible to "blow something out to space" because air current would only get you so far, especially with a cloud which is not a solid object, most you'd do is punch a hole or split it in two. Congratulations, it is now harder than impossible. Keeping it in the poles won't prevent the eventual expansion and enveloping of the earth, so it's just a waste of resources.

What's next? Nuking 'em? Spraying a thousand metric tonnes of salsa? Build a cult around it? Might as well, for all good it'll do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole “why didn’t the Foundation do some extremely specific thing” argument has never been particularly persuasive for me. It’s similar to the ultimately pointless variety of “why doesn’t the Foundation kill 682 by…” posts. If I wanted it to be contained, I would have written it differently and it would have been a different story. Also, planes with giant fans on them sound silly as hell.

I am still considering changes regarding the addenda structure and the presentation of low-level access.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see how it works. I see why it's a good idea. I could write an essay on why this is a textbook example of a unique, clever, and well-written SCP.

But I didn't enjoy it.

It wasn't actually fun to read. The unclickable addendums made me want to scream. The titles below were boring to read through, and ultimately, all that happened is what would be expected to happen if the Foundation failed to contain this SCP. Any Keter SCP could be written this way if you simply removed the Foundation's ability to contain it.

Here's an idea: use planes with giant fans to get it over one of the poles, and keep it there, if not blow it all the way into space. After all, if the air didn't affect it, it would just fall to the ground. It took me five seconds to think of that, and the Foubdation has thousands of people way, way smarter than me working 24/7 to come up with better stuff. Thus, I give it a -1 because it was unenjoyable and because it doesn't give the Foundation enough credit. I liked the writing before the end a whole lot, and I liked the concept a whole lot, so this doesn't reflect on your writing, only on the aspects which I explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well damn if this isn't the cleverest goddamn thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this for being a very interesting way to present a story, but I think I would like this more if it didn't disrupt electromagnetic waves or destroy matter: perpetual total cloudcover that doesn't rain would eventually result in most plant life dying, which would then cause total global ecosystem collapse.

I'm a sucker for those simple/mundane ends of the world, but this is really just a personal preference. Nothing stopping an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An excellent example of how doomsday skips should be done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty cool way of telling the story. Gives me strong SCP-579 (a long-time favorite of mine) vibes. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
19 iterations of SCP-3649 Special Containment Procedures have been archived and hidden.

This is chilling to me. And since I prefer shorter articles, that makes me like the fact that this is all that's stated even more.

+1 from me, here's to more short articles about how the end comes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I once read an absolutely devastating story where every line was a statistic, just a number and what the number represented.

This isn't exactly devastating, but I do love the unusual format. You don't always need prose to tell a story. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I left the page to do school related things and came back to review it later unaware of the fix :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the time you wrote this, there were no "Bait and switch" addenda (the plural of addendum).

The author updated them from the fake collapsibles nearly an hour ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate bait and switch Addendums so much. That ruined this for me

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, Another Scip that's good, but I don't want any more like this. The open question works once, and that's all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For all of my salty bitterness, I am in fact an optimist.

I believe in the fundamental goodness of humanity, and the ability of people to accomplish great things.

I'm actually not often dissapointed, contrary to popular belief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, and have to disagree that there's no flow or connection to it. I feel like the development of this thing (and the world itself) is told nicely through those document titles. I especially like the part where it implies that one of those GoIs (Serpents Hand?) breaks off a chunk of real-estate and declares themselves a country.
Anyway, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made a tiny edit to replace the empty collapsibles. In retrospect, I think they're more impolite to reader's expectations than they're worth in terms of what they convey indirectly.

Also, glad to see people are enjoying it! I can understand the complaint about it being somewhat disjointed, but I went into this recognizing that was the trade-off for how I wanted to write it overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this; I think part of what makes it work for me is how completely on-point a lot of your language sounds in regards to the titles for a lot of these documents. I can see how the collapsible thing is frustrating/annoying to others, though; I can also see how the disjointed style of the narrative doesn't work for others — but I'm a minimalist who loves SCPs with story-prompts, and this is one heck of a story prompt.

If you do end up heavily retooling it: Maybe make it only interfere with electromagnetic signals. This lulls the reader into a false sense of security until you note via document title that it's discovered it interferes even with the signals in people's brains (a literal "brain storm"). So when it lowers in altitude, it becomes a fog that pretty much destroys your ability to think rationally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Magnus the optimist, huh?
I think the documents are insinuating that Earth is dead, or at least civilization broke down, but I guess everyone has different headcanons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't say it better than Magnus. This article doesn't "flow"—instead of a movie, I was seeing disjointed frames, a slideshow of information related to each other but not directly. The format of the list also felt, idk, I almost downvoted right there and then because I didn't even consider how it might contribute to the story, and even after realizing it, it's unfortunately still a -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I’m being honest, I don’t think it works super well for the reasons Magnus mentioned. That said, it still gets an enthusiastic upvote from me for the creativity and innovation involved. It might not be to my personal taste, but I respect the hell out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's definitely something to consider! Thanks for the suggestion. Your outlook on what happened to the Foundation is a lot rosier than I expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would suggest instead of access denied, having the system be outdated, and see below documents.

I would also then suggest saying that the documents have been archived, see person xyz for access in ship abc, whatever. It also gives you a chance to show a touch of the narrative after terrestrial evacuation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still fairly split on the empty collapsible to be honest. I bounced between a lot of different ways to explain why the reader didn't have access to any of the documents noted later, and this way just the way to suggest incredibly low-level access that I settled on when nothing stood out as being the best (especially after I got warned off fake links, though this might not end up being any better.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you're going for, but there's too much dot, and not enough connection for me.

There's a lot of implied horror here, which was good, but the overall feel was lacking in my eyes.

-1 from me.

EDIT: Going back, there's no reason this shouldn't be on the wiki. It's something new, even if it's got a few weaknesses. By my own system, it gets an upvote from me. It still has all of the weaknesses I mentioned, and I think attempting to fix them would behoove you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hated this the first time I read it through, because that's my knee-jerk reaction when I see addendums with nothing in them, especially when they bait me into thinking there is. I still hate both of those first addendums.

But I read it again, because something just wasn't clicking and, well… the way that the third addendum presents the anomaly and all the implications about what it is, what it's doing, and what the Foundation has been trying to do about it, to no avail, is disconcerting to me in a very unique way. It's hard to fully put together what's going on here, but I've pieced together enough to tell myself 'Oh. That's not good. That's not good at all.'

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Getting eaten by a mass of clouds doesn't seem so bad…
Anyways, I like how we're supposed to infer what happened, which is fun.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the original, but this is a good take on it too, and certainlymore straightforward in it's implications, but not too much to give it all away.

item.title: Author Note
item.content:encoded:
Again, but with bureaucracy!

Thanks to Zyn and Petrograd for feedback at various stages.

Image source: https://www.flickr.com/photos/rocketjim54/7469923240

Translations:
Chinese: ashausesall
Japanese: C-Dives
